1.description: Trader earnings. This figure shows median trader earnings for participants in the bottom and top quartiles for Raven, CRT, and ToM scores. This figure also shows the bottom quartile, median, and top quartile for the whole distribution of participants’ earnings (see dashed horizontal lines).†p-values are based on panel regressions with random effects and robust standard errors clustered at the session level where the dependent variable is market earnings and the independent variable is a dummy whose value is one if the trader is in the top quartile of Raven, CRT, ToM, or all three variables.††In the first three comparisons, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile for a given skill (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of markets (17). In the last comparison, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile forallthree skills (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of markets (17). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Proportion of potentially profitable orders. This figure shows median proportions of potentially profitable orders for participants in the bottom and top quartiles for Raven, CRT, and ToM scores. This figure also shows the bottom quartile, median, and top quartile for the whole distribution of the proportion of potentially profitable orders (see dashed horizontal lines).†p-Values are based on panel regressions with random effects and robust standard errors clustered at the session level where the dependent variable is the proportion of potentially profitable orders and the independent variable is a dummy whose value is set to one if the trader is in the top quartile of Raven, CRT, ToM, or all three variables.††In the first three comparisons, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile for a given skill (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of markets in which they submitted an order. In the last comparison, the number of observations is equal to the number of subjects who are in either the bottom or the top quartile forallthree skills (Raven, CRT, or ToM) times the number of market periods in which they submitted an order. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
